text: This patient has moderately sized bilateral direct inguinal hernias.
The surgeon begins addressing the right inguinal hernia, focusing on the anatomical area where the hernia is located.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap approximately four to five centimeters from the superior border of the inguinal canal.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to effectively separate the peritoneum from the underlying preperitoneal structures, revealing the anatomical layers involved in the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels, ensuring they remain positioned on the anterior abdominal wall during the procedure.
The surgeon reduces the direct inguinal hernia, located medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, and exposes Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneal lining away from the gonadal vessels and spermatic cord, revealing these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal adipose tissue from the inguinal canal, ensuring that it does not mimic a hernia recurrence.
The surgeon prepares the space for mesh placement by enlarging the area, then shifts focus to the left inguinal hernia for further dissection and treatment.
The surgeon performs dissection using a sweeping motion, avoiding sharp dissection or electrocautery, as they continue to separate tissue layers in the inguinal region.
The surgeon visualizes the inferior epigastric vessels and reduces the direct inguinal hernia sac, which is located medial to these vessels.
The surgeon further develops the space of Retzius, connecting it to the right-sided dissection plane, expanding the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the triangle of pain, located lateral to the gonadal vessels, ensuring precise separation of the anatomical structures in this region.
However, remaining close to the peritoneal lining will prevent injury and severe post-operative pain.
The surgeon introduces the mesh into the adequately developed left inguinal space, preparing for placement within the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces ProGrip mesh into the surgical field, a self-fixating mesh that is visually positioned without the need for anchoring sutures or tacking devices.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Stratafix suture to close each peritoneal pocket, ensuring secure closure of the dissected areas.
There are many ways to arrange this mesh.
The surgeon orients the ProGrip mesh medially before unfolding it inferiorly within the developed left inguinal space.
The surgeon positions the inferior edge of the ProGrip mesh flush with the lower border of the dissected peritoneum, ensuring stability when the peritoneum is closed and re-approximated.
The surgeon repeats the placement of the ProGrip mesh on the contralateral side, ensuring it is positioned flush with the lower border of the dissected peritoneum.
In a narrow pelvis, the surgeon positions two pieces of mesh, which overlap at their medial ends, within the surgical field.
The surgeon arranges the mesh to cover all potential hernia defects and proceeds to close the peritoneal pockets in a running fashion using a 2-0 Stratafix suture.
The surgeon closes any defects in the peritoneal lining using Vicryl sutures to prevent intestinal adhesions, as the mesh lacks a protective barrier.
The surgeon uses a two O Stratafix suture to close the peritoneal pockets, ensuring the mesh remains securely in place.
It is absorbable and it is barbed, so it will not require knots.
The surgeon uses a suture that holds tension to facilitate the closure of the prepared needle pocket.
The surgeon removes the needles from the surgical site at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon places the excised bilateral cord lipomas into a specimen bag and removes them through an eight millimeter port.
Thank you for watching.
